title: ugetname
text: ugetname Return the name of the Windows object associated with a window handle. Explain this code #include <windows.h> unsigned char* ugetname (HWNDhWnd,unsigned char*name,intlen); Copy Parameters hWnd Window handle of a Microsoft Windows object within the calling Uniface application. name String that receives the name of the Windows object. len Maximum number of characters to be placed in the buffer pointed to by name . Because this does not include the zero terminator, the buffer should be defined one byte larger than the value of len . Return Values Table 1. Values returned by ugetname Value Meaning 0 The specified window handle is not recognized by Uniface. <>0 A pointer to the name of the window. This value is the memory address of the Name parameter. The exact contents of the string returned in name depend on the type of object indicated by hWnd . The possibilities are described in the following table. Table 2. Object names returned by ugetname Object Type Name Returned 4GL debug UDEBUG>ApplicationName Application ApplicationName Entity scroll bar UTDB.EntityName. ApplicationName Field FieldName.EntityName.ModelName.FormName. ApplicationName Form FormName.ApplicationName Form canvas FormName. ApplicationName Form panel PanelName.FormName. ApplicationName Form panel button ButtonText.PanelName.FormName.ApplicationName Message frame UFRAME.ApplicationName Message line UniCombo.ULINE.ApplicationName Pop-up panel PanelName.FormName.ApplicationName Session panel PanelName.ApplicationName Session panel button ButtonText.PanelName.ApplicationName Toolbar UTOOLBAR. ApplicationName Toolbar button ButtonText.UTOOLBAR. ApplicationName Note: If an application or form has a specific title, that title will be returned instead of the name. So, ApplicationName can also mean ApplicationTitle . The name returned for form panel buttons is language-dependent, that is, it corresponds to the Button Text information from the language setup, which can contain embedded spaces. Use Microsoft Windows platforms only. In library yrtl . Description ugetname returns the name of the Microsoft Windows object associated with the given window handle. You can get information only about Microsoft Windows objects that are part of the calling Uniface application. Note that the length of the supplied buffer must be one byte greater that the value specified by the len parameter. The type HWND is defined in the include file windows.h . Example: ugetname and ugethwnd The following example uses ugethwnd and ugetname to obtain the Uniface application window handle and title. It then changes the application title a couple of times (implementing a simple progress indicator) and finally restores it back to its original value. Explain this code #include <h3gl.h> #include <windows.h> EXPORT void PHASECOUNTER () { HWND hApp; char name[201]; char txt[50]; int i; hApp = ugethwnd(); /* Get application window handle */ ugetname(hApp, name, sizeof(name)-1); /* Get window title */ for ( i=1; i<10; i++ ) /* During 10 seconds ... */ { sprintf(txt, "Phase %d of 10", i); SetWindowText(hApp, txt); /* Change application window title */ Sleep(1000); /* Pause 1 second */ } SetWindowText(hApp, name); /* Restore application window title*/ } Copy Was this topic helpful?
